1989_10_29-India_Mumbai_ISKCON_Chowpatty-Diwali_Address-Holy_Day_Not_Holiday-st2-vr01

Radhanath Swami Transcriptions Template

Meta Data v9

Event Name/Title

Holy Day Not Holiday

Event Date

29 Oct 1989

Event Location

India, Mumbai, ISKCON Chowpatty

Event Type

Temple - Festival

Transcriber

Vinaya Nimai das, Lakshmi Vijaya devi dasi

Completed

22 Nov 2021

Source Audio

https://www.radhanathswamimedia.com/Lec/1989/89-074-Holyday%20Not%20Holiday%20(Mumbai)-OT-dt_89_10_29.mp3

Source Video

NA

Verse

NA

Tags – Places

Ayodhya, America, India, Bharat

Tags – Pastimes

Lord Ram being banished to forest, Lord Ram's Return to Ayodhya

Tags – Persons

Ram, Lakshman, Sita, Hanuman, Dasharath, Kaikeyi, Manthara, Bharat

Tags – Themes

Festival, Celebration, Holiday, Auspiciousness, Devotion, Diwali, Christmas, association, goal of life, exchange of gifts,

Lecture Transcription

So, this is the time of the year where many people spend sleepless nights listening to the sound vibration of explosions in the atmosphere, the auspicious invocation of the union between fire and gunpowder. Ah! And it is all in the name of celebrating. It is also the time of the year when people visit one another to offer best wishes. Of course, it's usually so many superficial formalities but every now and then there's a sincere exchange, and of course there is a sense of happiness and joy wherever there is meeting of old friends and wherever there is the mood of celebration. But we should understand that human life is meant for self-realization and these auspicious days were originally intended exclusively and only for that purpose.

Meaning and significance of Holiday

Holiday is an English word which is a combination of two words holy and day. Ah! Holy day means the day that is very sacred, in that it is a day that commemorates the glory of God, it is a day when people come together to remind each other of the holiness of that day and help each other to come closer to God.

Christmas means Christ-Mass

In America the holiest day is Christmas. Mass is a celebration in the church you know Mass, they have; it is a very sacred holy ritual which is supposed to unite the soul or the consciousness of the aspiring devotee with the body and the spirit of Christ. Christ-Mass means that auspicious day when Jesus Christ appeared in this world and it's a day when we come together as families, as communities to unite our consciousness again, with the body and spirit of God. Of course, Christmas today is rarely ever seen in that way. It is a day of exchanging gifts for us for our own sense gratification. It is a day when businessmen usually make half the profits of their year. That is a fact. The Christmas season, most businesses retailers, they make half their profits for the whole year during the month and a half before Christmas because everyone is so anxious to buy gifts and every gift is sense gratification. Ah! It is a day when they eat a dead animal, the ham, and generally, it is a day to celebrate by taking intoxication. They’re completely forgetting Jesus. In fact, some people are so honest that they have literally taken Christ out of Christmas and more common in… in America than the word Christmas is now Xmas. They’ve literally ‘X’ed out Jesus Christ and put ‘X’, X-mas! you have heard this? [devotee:] Yes. That means they want the celebration for their fun, but they don't want to be reminded of the spiritual significance of the day.

Diwali is a very holy day

Similarly, Diwali is a very holy day. It is a very…very high spiritual day and it is one of those auspicious times when one can make tremendous amounts of spiritual advancement and for this reason the day is meant to be celebrated. Of course, the unity of friends and families is very sweet and very nice but originally this unification of coming together is meant to help one another to come closer to the lord. Lord Ramchandra, as you know the story, by the influence of his… one of his mothers, Kaikeyi, he was banished to the forest for fourteen years. Dasharath Maharaj promised Kaikeyi at one time on the battlefield, ah! Three wishes. I'm sorry two wishes and being a great king, he was obliged. So those two wishes that she desired under the influence of Manthara, an evil-minded woman. She desired that Ram be banished to the forest for fourteen years and Bharat be king. This is very significant. Kaikeyi was a very virtuous, very pious and a very noble, religious woman. In fact, in many ways, she was the favourite wife of King Dasharath. When he would go on the battlefield she would often go and drive his chariot and help him fight. This was her dedication to her husband. But even such a noble woman as Kaikeyi, because she had association with evil-minded person, she lost all of her good intelligence.

The influence of association

Kaikeyi was far more spiritually elevated than any of us here in this room today, and the message of the Ramayan is just see even such elevated persons as Kaikeyi, if she associates with materialistic envious evil-minded persons, they lose their intelligence. There is nothing so important in our life as saintly association and there is nothing more destructive in our life than association with sinful people.

Lord Chaitanya has said asat-saṅga-tyāga ei vaiṣṇava-ācāra [Cc. Madhya 22.87], that if you want to be Krishna conscious, if you want to become a lover of God, it is very simple, associate with sadhus and do not associate with materialistic people intimately. If you associate with materialistic people intimately, you have no chance of becoming God conscious. And if you associate with saintly persons then you are guaranteed to become God conscious. So, our life is dependent on who we associate with. If we think that we are very pious, we are very noble, we are very advanced, but we can still get away with having intimate association with materialistic people then compare yourself to Kaikeyi. She was better than you and just see what happened to her. Simply by the association of this Manthara, she condemned the Supreme Lord Ram, who she loved more than her own son. Factually Kaikeyi, she loved Ram even more than Bharat. They had such a deep loving relationship but due to the influence of Maya, she cast him away! But actually, we have all done the same thing.

We have all had in the past, an intimate relationship with Lord Ram. In the kingdom of our heart, we are meant to love Ram and serve Ram with great intimacy and devotion, but due to evil association in this world we have banished Ram from our heart. Have not we? That is why we are in this material world. That is why we are facing all the problems of life, because we have literally banished God from our heart. Although he's there we can't get him out of there. In our...we have banished him from our consciousness. Now if we want him back, there's a process, to associate with those persons who want him back. Ah! That's all! Very simple.

Mood of Ayodhya residents

So, Lord Ramchandra, when the news came to the residents of Ayodhya that he was to leave for fourteen years, this news was worse than death. Every man, woman, and child, even the animals, the insects, the trees, the grass, every living entity in Ayodhya was cent percent devoted to the service of Ram. They were willing to give their lives for the slightest pleasure that they could offer to Lord Shri Ram. He was their life and soul. They knew nothing except Sita and Ram. When the news came to them that Ram was about to leave, it was a sword that pierced their heart and caused them so much pain that they were bleeding in the ocean of lamentation. You see, the citizens of Ayodhya at every moment their only desire was to see Ram and whatever time they weren't seeing him they were simply anticipating when will the moment be when I can see him, when I can hear him when I can be with him. This was their moment after moment meditation, twenty-four hours a day. The thought of not seeing him for fourteen years was millions of times more painful than death. The thought of of him, the prince of Ayodhya living in the jungles with the wild animals, filled with rakshasas, living on the ground, with barefoot stepping on the thorns then worse than that the beloved Janaki, Sita Devi, following behind, it was so unbearable that they all begged Ram, ‘We will go to the forest, we will take on your behalf, this curse and you stay here and enjoy with Sita in Ayodhya.’ Lord Shri Ram being a loyal and true son to his father, he said, “No, no I must go, and you must stay!” We know it is a very long and very deeply emotional story of what took place when Lord Ram was leaving Ayodhya. People were fainting, some people were dying, there was just incessant tears flooding the earth as Lord Shri Ramchandra’s chariot drove toward Dandakaranya forest.

At one time, headed by Bharat, all the citizens of Ayodhya practically, they went to Chitrakut, to beg Ram to return. Bharat, he pleaded, “let me take this curse, you go home!” but Ram was very firm in carrying out his father's last wish. From that day on, for the next fourteen years, the citizens of Ayodhya were simply meditating on the day Ram would return with Sita and Lakshman. It was impossible for them to think of anything else. Just like when a young man loves a young woman or a young woman loves a young man, there's a very very deep and great anticipation that you want to be with, you want to see your beloved, one moment seems so long in their absence. Meditate that infinitely and you will get some idea of the intensity of the separation and at the absorption in that separation of the residents of Ayodhya. They were simply crying for Ram. Day and night, that fourteen years seemed like millions and millions and millions and millions of lifetimes of Brahma to the residents of Ayodhya. They simply passed their time chanting his holy names, worshiping the beautiful form of his murti, and discussing amongst one another his beautiful glories and pastimes. It was only through this process that they could maintain their lives. But as that fourteen years started coming closer and closer to an end, they were feeling more and more hope, but then the news came to them; in the last few years when their expectations were at the heights, the news came that the demon Ravana has stolen away Sita. The most horrible powerful ten-headed Rakshasa, who was famous for drinking the blood of human beings and destroying the greatest conquerors, had snatched away that…that gentle, shy, beautiful, little helpless princess Janaki. And how they were crying ‘will we ever see our Sita again?’ The residents of Ayodhya, lived constantly in the anxiety of separation ‘when will Ram come home? when will Sita come home?’

The goal of life

It has said that the price of knowing God, the price of having God, is the tears you shed in separation from him. If we want to become devotees of the lord, we must follow in the footsteps of the residents of Ayodhya because it is a fact, the lord has been banished by our own enviousness from our consciousness, for many many many births. And how much we should be longing, how much we should be praying for the lord to return. How much we should be in complete anxiety for the day when he returns, when that love, when that devotion again awakens within our heart. Huh! this is the goal of life, to cultivate that separation from the lord, to cultivate that longing to again love Ram, to again... because Ram will appear directly in accordance to [with] our love for him within our lives.

So of course, we know the beautiful story of how Lord Shri Ramchandra along with the help of Sugriva, Hanuman, Angada, Jambavan and the other monkey soldiers, how they defeated the entire rakshasa army, how he slayed Ravana, and how again he attained the hand of Sita. Just after Ram conquered Lanka and returned Sita, again obtained the hand of Sita, just at that time the fourteen years had ended so it was time for them to return to Ayodhya. Seated on a beautiful flower airplane, they traveled through the airways and the news came. Hanumanji went ahead to tell all the residents of Ayodhya, the news that they'd been longing for moment after moment; that Ram, Sita and Lakshman are on their way home. There was no greater jubilation in their life. It was like the soul returning to a dead corpse. All of their expectations, all of their anticipations, all of their longings, all of their sufferings, everything was about to be fulfilled by the presence of Ram. Everyone in that city of Ayodhya, they spent days and days and days decorating the city to celebrate his arrival. They took these coloured powders, rangoli, and they decorated all the streets with beautiful colours and each family decorated the front of their house, decorated their entire house inside and out, so that when Ram would pass, he would feel ‘Ah!’ that, ‘Again I am home.’ They decorated their houses in such a way because they knew that Ram would enter into each and every one of their homes, because he was their all-merciful friend and lord of their life. And they with the finest ghee from the cows, they put it in little lights, in little lamps and with the ghee wick. There were literally millions and millions and millions of these little ghee lamps on each roof of each house, in each doorway of each house, alongside the roads, to light the way for Ram, to show him their love and to welcome them home, welcome him home again into their hearts. And Ram came and when he saw the beauty of Ayodhya, and he saw the expressions of the love the residents of Ayodhya through their songs, through the food that they offered him and through the beautiful decorations. There was a very very...it was an infinite, inconceivable reunion of joy. It cannot be expressed in words. Never has the world seen such a celebration, such happiness, such joy.

Real purpose of Diwali

Diwali is the celebration of welcoming Ram back into our lives, into our homes, into our families. Diwali is meant to be a meditation on the sentiments of the residents of Ayodhya at the time Ram was returning. That is the purpose of this day. Not that we decorate our house, just to show that we can decorate our house very nicely, better than our neighbours. The purpose is to decorate our house to invite God to enter, to make it a beautiful, wonderful, glorious place for Ram to return, to make our home into the home of Ram. This is your home Ram, you have been away from my life for so long, please come back! The lamp of my love is lit, inviting you back! And friends, family members, acquaintances, they gather, together to celebrate the joyous appearance of Ram in their life, to celebrate that Ram has come home again, into our life, into our heart and into our home. Believe me, this is far more joyful, meaningful, and blissful than superficially shaking hands and lighting firecrackers. Unfortunately, our society has taken the most holy and the most sacred, opportunities to develop our love of God and to develop our closeness to God and made them into secular occasions which have no meaning beyond some temporary sense gratification. Ah! But it is our duty as friends to remind one another and take advantage of this most… most precious opportunity. Today is the day when each, and every one of us should be meditative... meditating, contemplating, and pondering upon the mood of the residents of Ayodhya; what they went through for fourteen years and how happy they were when Ram returned. Today is the day when we should meditate upon the loss, that we have inflicted upon ourselves by banishing Ram from our own lives and substituting him with a world, with a life simply full of material considerations. Today is a day when we should be meditating from the bottom of our heart, on how important it is to light the lamp of our love within our own heart to attract Ram to come back to us. Ah! This is the meaning of Diwali. And Ram will come! Krishna says in Bhagavad-Gita,

man-manā bhava mad-bhakto
mad-yājī māṁ namaskuru
mām evaiṣyasi yuktvaivam
ātmānaṁ mat-parāyaṇaḥ

[Bg. 9.34]

[Engage your mind always in thinking of Me, become My devotee, offer obeisances to Me and worship Me. Being completely absorbed in Me, surely you will come to Me.]

‘Always think of me, become my devotee, worship me and offer your homage unto me. And this way you will come to me without fail.’

The purpose of Holidays

The purpose of these sacred holy days is to put everything else aside. You know originally, why do you think on holidays or holy days you didn't work? It wasn't so you could sit home and watch the cricket match. The reason why you had the day off is you were supposed to exclusively dedicate that [those] twenty-four hours, every minute to remembering God on this holy day, his day, the day of his appearance. If you're not doing that, it's better if you go on working. Why take the day off, simply for leisure? That's not the purpose of a holy day, to have leisure. The purpose of a holy day, a holiday is to intensely, completely, immerse your consciousness. Forget about eating and sleeping, forgetting about every other consideration in your life except your need to remember God. And to make a grand festival, a wonderful feast a wonderful celebration in the temple or in your home, to celebrate the glory of the appearance of God in this world and in your life, that is the purpose of these holy days.

India is the land of Dharma. Bharat varsha is the place that is supposed to guide the whole world back to the spiritual world.

bhārata-bhūmite haila manuṣya janma yāra
janma sārthaka kari’ kara para-upakāra

[Cc. Adi 9.41]

[One who has taken his birth as a human being in the land of India [Bhārata-varṣa] should make his life successful and work for the benefit of all other people.]

The Supreme Lord Himself said ‘every person who is born in Bharat varsha or India, it is his responsibility to live such a God conscious life, that the whole world will follow in his footsteps.’

Now in the western world, to celebrate holidays by simply engaging in eating meat, drinking intoxications, and exchanging gifts of sense gratification, it's expected. But in the land of dharma, Bharat varsha, it is a great embarrassment. Huh! By our birthright, we we are obliged to understand, what is our responsibility in life, what are the spiritual virtues that we have inherited in this holy land. Aha! It is a very serious matter, and it is the most glorious opportunity. There is nothing so joyous, there is nothing so enlightening, illuminating and refreshing than having a grand celebration to glorify God, to glorify the return of God into our lives. Ah!

Happy Diwali! Hare Krishna! Thank you very much!

Q&A Transcription

[Devotee]: What about other national holidays like Independence Day, Republic Day?

[Maharaja]: These, these are not... this is just a very loose term of the word. Diwali is actually a holy day. Republic Day, it depends on your vision; whether it's holy or not. Days… days based on particular political references in history are not very holy. But we should know that sarva-loka-maheśvaram [Bg. 5.29], everything is the property of God. Republic Day, this is God's republic. Aha! It is not India's republic; it is God’s republic. He is the proprietor of everything and everyone, we should remember that. Ah! Independence Day, we should understand what is independence? We should meditate that independence means no more birth, old age, disease, and death. And the only way to attain that according to Krishna is, yaṁ yaṁ vāpi smaran bhāvaṁ [Bg. 8.6], anta-kāle ca mām eva smaran muktvā kalevaram [Bg. 8.5], ‘one who remembers me at the time of death never takes birth again in this world. He attains independence.’ Ah!

And the only way you'll remember Krishna at the time of death; if you remember him during your life at every moment, right? So, on Independence Day you should prepare yourself for independence [chuckles], by remembering Krishna. So, in this way whatever day you mention we’ll find a way that you can always remember God. [chuckles]That is the purpose of life, and these auspicious days are very very special opportunities for that purpose only.

Yes?

[Devotee]: When we go out on Janmashtami celebration or any other celebration, to ask some donation, we find that people are very difficult to handle. On Diwali day, we find the same people, sending lot of gifts, sweets or various other presentations through their drivers, their office bearers at our place. So how should we react to this situation?

[Maharaja]: You should take full advantage of it, for their benefit. [chuckles] For their benefit. Don't be shy. [laughter] There's a saying in English when the iron is hot strike it, don't wait. [chuckles] They're in a giving mood, by all means let them give to God. Ah! Actually, Diwali is the day when everyone is meant to give gifts to Ram. Ayodhya, they weren't make... they were giving gifts to Ram. Christmas is a day, it's Christ's birthday, it's not your birthday. [laughter]Why? Just like if it's…if it's your birthday and everyone's giving gifts to everyone, everyone's giving gifts to each other, but nobody gives anything to you, what do you gonna [going to] think? Right? If it's your birthday and I'm expecting everyone to give me a present, what are you going to think about me? You're going to think, I'm not a very good friend, right? So, it's Jesus Christ's birthday, Christmas, and everyone is simply giving everyone gifts except Jesus, right? Similarly, Diwali is the day when Ram comes home. It's the day when everyone in Ayodhya was presenting gifts to him, right? Of course, giving gifts to one another is also very good. It's a sweet exchange. It should not be... that should not be stopped; giving gifts to one another. But the gifts to one another should be with the spirit of the day to help each other, gifts that should remind each other in love of the purpose of life, huh? Not a bottle of champagne! How's that going to help you remember Ram? So, giving gifts to one another is a very good thing, it's an exchange of love. But those gifts should be out of love to help one to capture the spirit of the real… of the celebration of the day, which is to glorify Ram. Ha? But more than concern with gifts for one another, we should be thinking how on this day will I glorify Ram? What can I give to him? What can I give to help his divine cause of love to all living beings? Ha! That is the greatest thing!

Radha Gopinath is non-different than Ram and Sita. Sita and Ram have appeared to us today in their… in their beautiful…ava… in their beautiful forms of Sri Sri Radha Gopinath. Here God is sitting right before us, Sita and Ram. The day of Diwali is the day to present gifts to them. Since you asked the question, I'll be very honest. I'm not demanding or instructing anything. I'm just... something to consider in your life, all the money and all the energy you've meant…you…you… you've dedicated to giving gifts to others and cards and everything to others; what will you give to Sita and Ram? It's their day! Ah! The expression of our love, it's not the gift. Just like my mother; when I was a little child, when her birthday would come, I would never know what to give my mother. I don't know. First of all, throughout my life, I never knew what to do in regard to women. That's why I took sannyas. So, what to speak of even my own mother, I never knew what gift to get her. So, her birthday would come, I'd say, “mother what can I get you? I don't know what to get you.” she'd say, “you get me anything you like.” I’d say, “I don't know what I like.” She said, “it doesn't matter what you give me, it's the thought that counts. I don't need anything you give me, it's the thought. The fact that you've went through an endeavour, you raised money, you did everything to buy something, and you tried to get the best thing you possibly could. Even if I don't like it, it's the thought that counts, I’ll like it!” Right? It's the love. People who are attached to the external gift are very shallow people. The gift is an expression of love. It's the love that gives joy, right? But that love, if…if it's your birthday and someone says ‘well, I don't… I don't… I don't want to spend any money or any time, but I'll give you, my love!’ You’ll think ‘well, what kind of love is that?’ right? Well, that's the way God thinks about you. That you give everybody else nice gifts but when it comes to God, ‘God, I'll give you my love, but I won't give you anything else.’ Right? God is a person. Ram is a person. Gopinath is a person. So, we manifest the expression of our love by giving gift. Diwali means give gifts to Ram and Sita. You give to everyone else but most of all it's his day. But unfortunately, we will go through weeks and weeks of preparation of gifts and cards and everything for everyone else but when it comes to Radha Gopinath, everyone thinks ‘ah! I'm sorry, I forgot!’ Right? That means we have wasted our human life. [chuckles] What do you think? Is there any other question?

Jai Sita Ram!

Quotes

Holiday is an English word which is a combination of two words holy and day. Holy day means the day that is very sacred, in that it is a day that commemorates the glory of God, it is a day when people come together to remind each other of the holiness of that day and help each other to come closer to God.

There is nothing so important in our life as saintly association and there is nothing more destructive in our life than association with sinful people.

If you associate with materialistic people intimately you have no chance of becoming God conscious. And if you associate with saintly persons then you are guaranteed to become God conscious. So, our life is dependent on who we associate with.

the price of knowing God, the price of having God, is the tears you shed in separation from him.

The purpose of a holy day, a holiday is to intensely, completely, immerse your consciousness. Forget about eating and sleeping, forgetting about every other consideration in your life except your need to remember God. And to make a grand festival, a wonderful feast a wonderful celebration in the temple or in your home, to celebrate the glory of the appearance of God in this world and in your life, that is the purpose of these holy days.
